issues.original: If your team trains or serves any model on Alibaba Cloud, sooner or later you will end up in the PAI console. PAI is the umbrella; underneath it sit the actual workhorses — a notebook product, a distributed training service, a model-serving service, plus a couple of GUI/quick-deploy layers on top.
issues.fixed: If your team trains or serves any model on Alibaba Cloud, you'll eventually use the PAI console. PAI is the umbrella; underneath it are the actual workhorses — a notebook product, a distributed training service, a model-serving service, and a few GUI/quick-deploy layers.

issues.original: After about eighteen months of running real LLM workloads on it for an AI marketing platform, this series is the field guide I wish someone had handed me before I shipped my first endpoint.
issues.fixed: After about eighteen months of running real LLM workloads on it for an AI marketing platform, this series is the field guide I wish I had before shipping my first endpoint.

issues.original: The goal here is so that when I say "DLC job" or "EAS endpoint" later you already know what bucket they fall into.
issues.fixed: The goal is to help you understand what "DLC job" and "EAS endpoint" mean.

issues.original: Per the official docs, **Platform for AI (PAI)** is "Alibaba Cloud's AI development platform covering the full lifecycle: data annotation, model development, training, and deployment". The console at [CODE] is one entry point, but PAI itself is a *family* of related products that share an account model, an OSS-backed storage layer, and a single Python SDK.
issues.fixed: According to the official docs, **Platform for AI (PAI)** is Alibaba Cloud's AI development platform covering the full lifecycle: data annotation, model development, training, and deployment. The console at [CODE] is one entry point, but PAI itself is a family of related products that share an account model, an OSS-backed storage layer, and a single Python SDK.

issues.original: The mental model that has worked best for me: - **PAI is the shop.** - **DSW, DLC, EAS, Designer, Model Gallery** are the workbenches inside. - **ECS, OSS, NAS, CPFS** are where the actual silicon and bytes live. PAI just orchestrates them on your behalf.
issues.fixed: The mental model that works best for me: - **PAI is the shop.** - **DSW, DLC, EAS, Designer, Model Gallery** are the workbenches inside. - **ECS, OSS, NAS, CPFS** are where the actual silicon and bytes live. PAI orchestrates them for you.

issues.original: Read that bottom-up. The infrastructure layer is the silicon — CPUs, GPUs, RDMA fabric, and ACK Kubernetes underneath. On top of that, *Lingjun* (灵骏) gives you very-high-density AI compute and *general-purpose compute* gives you everyday ECS-backed GPU pools. The platform-and-tools layer is where you spend your day: PyTorch / Megatron / DeepSpeed, plus PAI's optimization toys (TorchAcc, BladeLLM, EasyCkpt, AIMaster), plus the visible products (DSW, DLC, EAS, Designer, FeatureStore, iTAG). The application layer is how PAI plugs into the rest of Alibaba's MaaS world (ModelScope, Bailian/DashScope, Model Studio). The business layer is the marketing slide for industry use cases.
issues.fixed: Read it from the bottom up. The infrastructure layer includes CPUs, GPUs, RDMA fabric, and ACK Kubernetes. Above that, *Lingjun* (灵骏) provides high-density AI compute, while *general-purpose compute* offers everyday ECS-backed GPU pools. The platform-and-tools layer is where you spend most of your time: PyTorch, Megatron, DeepSpeed, PAI's optimization tools (TorchAcc, BladeLLM, EasyCkpt, AIMaster), and the visible products (DSW, DLC, EAS, Designer, FeatureStore, iTAG). The application layer integrates PAI with Alibaba's MaaS world (ModelScope, Bailian/DashScope, Model Studio). The business layer covers industry use cases.

issues.original: The reason to use PAI instead of raw ECS is that it pre-bakes the CUDA / PyTorch images, mounts your OSS bucket for you, gives you a metrics dashboard, and bills per second.
issues.fixed: Use PAI instead of raw ECS because it pre-configures CUDA and PyTorch images, mounts your OSS bucket, provides a metrics dashboard, and bills per second.

issues.original: There's also **iTAG** (data annotation), **PAI-Lingjun** for very large clusters, **PAI-Blade / BladeLLM** for inference optimization, and **FeatureStore**, but unless you're doing >1000-GPU pretraining or building a recommender system, you can ignore them on day one.
issues.fixed: There's also **iTAG** (data annotation), **PAI-Lingjun** for large clusters, **PAI-Blade / BladeLLM** for inference optimization, and **FeatureStore**. Unless you're doing >1000-GPU pretraining or building a recommender system, you can ignore them initially.

issues.original: This trips up everyone who comes from a pure cloud-VM background. Three rules: 1. **PAI never owns your data.** Datasets, checkpoints, and model artifacts all live in **OSS** (or **NAS** for POSIX semantics, or **CPFS** for HPC-style throughput). When a DSW or DLC instance dies, anything you didn't write to OSS is gone. There is a "system disk" but treat it as [CODE].
issues.fixed: This often confuses people coming from a pure cloud-VM background. Here are three rules: 1. **PAI never owns your data.** Datasets, checkpoints, and model artifacts all live in **OSS** (or **NAS** for POSIX semantics, or **CPFS** for HPC-style throughput). If a DSW or DLC instance dies, anything not written to OSS is lost. There is a "system disk," but treat it as [CODE].

issues.original: 2. **PAI does own the compute.** You do not provision GPU ECS instances yourself for PAI workloads. PAI manages a pool, you ask for [CODE] and you get billed per second of allocation.
issues.fixed: 2. **PAI owns the compute.** You don't need to provision GPU ECS instances for PAI workloads. PAI manages a pool, and you get billed per second of allocation.

issues.original: 3. **PAI shares your account but uses its own RAM roles.** When you grant PAI access to OSS, you're attaching a service-linked role ([CODE]) so PAI's compute can read your bucket without a long-lived AK pair. Do not skip this — without it your DLC jobs will fail at [CODE] time with a 403.
issues.fixed: 3. **PAI shares your account but uses its own RAM roles.** Granting PAI access to OSS involves attaching a service-linked role ([CODE]), allowing PAI's compute to read your bucket without a long-lived AK pair. Don't skip this step — without it, your DLC jobs will fail with a 403 error.

issues.original: To get started you need three things in this order: 1. **An aliyun.com account** with real-name verification (实名认证) — required for any GPU resource. International accounts work for most regions but Hangzhou, Shanghai, and Beijing have the best GPU stock. 2. **A region.** Pick one and stick to it. PAI resources, OSS buckets, and ECS GPUs are all region-scoped, and cross-region traffic costs money and adds latency. For mainland production I default to [CODE]; for international, [CODE] (Singapore). 3. **A workspace.** Per the docs, the workspace is PAI's tenancy primitive — it holds quotas, datasets, model registries, and IAM bindings. You almost always want at least two: a [CODE] workspace where humans poke around in DSW, and a [CODE] workspace where DLC jobs and EAS endpoints live. Cross-workspace permissioning is fiddly, but the isolation pays for itself the first time an intern accidentally restarts a serving endpoint.
issues.fixed: To get started, you need three things in this order: 1. **An aliyun.com account** with real-name verification (实名认证) — required for any GPU resource. International accounts work for most regions, but Hangzhou, Shanghai, and Beijing have the best GPU stock. 2. **A region.** Choose one and stick to it. PAI resources, OSS buckets, and ECS GPUs are region-specific, and cross-region traffic incurs costs and adds latency. For mainland production, I default to [CODE]; for international, [CODE] (Singapore). 3. **A workspace.** According to the docs, the workspace is PAI's tenancy primitive, holding quotas, datasets, model registries, and IAM bindings. You usually need at least two: a [CODE] workspace for DSW, and a [CODE] workspace for DLC jobs and EAS endpoints. Cross-workspace permissioning is complex, but the isolation is worth it, especially if an intern accidentally restarts a serving endpoint.

issues.original: Every time I onboard a new ML engineer to PAI the first day looks the same. They start a DSW instance, [CODE] their world, train for an hour, restart the kernel for some reason, and then ask me where their model file went. The honest answer — "in [CODE] on a node that no longer exists" — is the kind of lesson you only need to learn once. This article is the version of that lesson you read in advance.
issues.fixed: Every time I onboard a new ML engineer to PAI, the first day is the same. They start a DSW instance, [CODE] their world, train for an hour, restart the kernel for some reason, and then ask me where their model file went. The honest answer — "in [CODE] on a node that no longer exists" — is a lesson you only need to learn once. This article is the version of that lesson you read in advance.

issues.original: Per the official "DSW Overview", DSW is a **cloud-based IDE for AI development** that integrates JupyterLab, VSCode, and a terminal, with pre-configured container images for PyTorch and TensorFlow, heterogeneous compute (CPU / GPU / Lingjun), and the ability to mount datasets from OSS, NAS, and CPFS. In practice that means you click "Open" and within a minute you have a real Jupyter on a real GPU with [CODE] working and PyTorch already importable.
issues.fixed: According to the official "DSW Overview," DSW is a **cloud-based IDE for AI development** that integrates JupyterLab, VSCode, and a terminal, with pre-configured container images for PyTorch and TensorFlow, heterogeneous compute (CPU / GPU / Lingjun), and the ability to mount datasets from OSS, NAS, and CPFS. In practice, this means you click "Open" and within a minute, you have a real Jupyter on a real GPU with [CODE] working and PyTorch already importable.

issues.original: What's interesting is *what's not in the box*. The DSW container has a system disk that lives only as long as the instance does. Anything you [CODE] survives a kernel restart but does not survive an instance restart unless you persist the conda env to OSS or save it to ACR via the snapshot feature.
issues.fixed: What's interesting is *what's not in the box*. The DSW container has a system disk that only lasts as long as the instance. Anything you [CODE] survives a kernel restart but not an instance restart unless you persist the conda env to OSS or save it to ACR via the snapshot feature.

issues.original: Per the docs, DSW resource types come in two flavors: **public resources** (pay-as-you-go) and **dedicated resources** (subscription on general-purpose compute or Lingjun). For day-to-day work, public is the right answer — you're paying for the GPU minutes you actually use, and the per-second metering means you can spin one up for a 10-minute experiment and not care.
issues.fixed: According to the docs, DSW resource types come in two flavors: **public resources** (pay-as-you-go) and **dedicated resources** (subscription on general-purpose compute or Lingjun). For day-to-day work, public resources are the right choice — you pay for the GPU minutes you actually use, and the per-second metering lets you spin one up for a 10-minute experiment without worry.

issues.original: The console flow looks like this: The lifecycle hooks are easy to ignore and expensive to forget. **Idle shutdown** at 30 minutes is my default; **scheduled shutdown** at 11pm catches the case where I leave a notebook running over the weekend. Every DSW idle GPU at 5 RMB an hour is roughly 100 RMB you owe Aliyun by Monday.
issues.fixed: The console flow looks like this: The lifecycle hooks are easy to ignore but costly to forget. **Idle shutdown** at 30 minutes is my default; **scheduled shutdown** at 11pm catches the case where I leave a notebook running over the weekend. Each idle DSW GPU costs 5 RMB an hour, which adds up to about 100 RMB owed to Aliyun by Monday.

issues.original: The moment you need eight GPUs across two nodes, or the moment training runs longer than the eight hours you'll keep the tab open, you switch to **DLC**.
issues.fixed: When you need eight GPUs across two nodes, or when training runs longer than eight hours, you switch to **DLC**.

issues.original: The docs call this *Deep Learning Containers*; we just say "DLC job".
issues.fixed: The docs call this *Deep Learning Containers*; we just say "DLC job".

issues.original: The first and third bullets are what make DLC interesting compared to renting GPU ECS yourself.
issues.fixed: The first and third points are what make DLC interesting compared to renting GPU ECS yourself.

issues.original: Two of those phases — **scheduler places pods** and **mount OSS / NAS** — are where almost all of the "my job is stuck in PENDING" tickets get filed. Stuck on schedule means your resource group is full; stuck on mount means your storage RAM role is wrong. Same as DSW, the diagnostic move is to spin up a tiny DSW with the same OSS mount and confirm [CODE] works.
issues.fixed: Two of these phases — **scheduler places pods** and **mount OSS / NAS** — are where most "my job is stuck in PENDING" tickets come from. If it's stuck on scheduling, your resource group is full. If it's stuck on mounting, your storage RAM role is incorrect. Just like with DSW, the diagnostic step is to spin up a small DSW with the same OSS mount and confirm [CODE] works.

issues.original: For most teams the answer is general-purpose, pay-as-you-go. Lingjun makes sense once you're training above 8 GPUs and need RDMA between nodes — the docs note RDMA is configurable on Lingjun and gives "accelerated inter-node communication" (which is a polite way of saying NCCL AllReduce will be 5-10x faster than over standard ethernet). Preemptible is a cost saver for jobs that checkpoint cleanly, which thanks to EasyCKPT is most jobs.
issues.fixed: For most teams, the best choice is general-purpose, pay-as-you-go. Lingjun is useful when you're training with more than 8 GPUs and need RDMA between nodes. The docs mention that RDMA is configurable on Lingjun and provides "accelerated inter-node communication" (meaning NCCL AllReduce will be 5-10x faster than over standard Ethernet). Preemptible is a cost-effective option for jobs that checkpoint cleanly, which, thanks to EasyCKPT, is true for most jobs.

issues.fixed: The console "Training Jobs" view provides logs, GPU utilization, network throughput, and AIMaster events. You can also stream logs from the SDK:

issues.original: For longer jobs I forward logs to SLS (Log Service) and set CloudMonitor alerts on [CODE] for 15 minutes — that is the canonical signal that something is wedged on data loading or distributed init.
issues.fixed: For longer jobs, I forward logs to SLS (Log Service) and set CloudMonitor alerts on [CODE] for 15 minutes — this is the canonical signal that something is stuck on data loading or distributed init.

issues.original: AIMaster runs as a sidecar pod alongside your training pods, with its own ServiceAccount that has the right K8s permissions to mark, evict, and recreate worker pods. It does three things:
issues.fixed: AIMaster runs as a sidecar pod alongside your training pods, with its own ServiceAccount that has the necessary K8s permissions to mark, evict, and recreate worker pods. It performs three tasks:

issues.original: EAS is where the money goes. DSW costs you a few hundred RMB a month for dev. DLC costs you in spikes. EAS bills 24/7 because someone might call your endpoint, and that "minimum replica count" line in the autoscaler config is the single highest-leverage knob in the whole platform. This article is what I wish I'd known the day before we shipped our first production endpoint.
issues.fixed: EAS is where the money goes. DSW costs a few hundred RMB a month for development. DLC costs spike. EAS bills 24/7 because someone might call your endpoint, and the "minimum replica count" in the autoscaler config is the most critical setting in the entire platform. This article covers what I wish I'd known before shipping our first production endpoint.

issues.original: The official "EAS overview" frames it as: "deploy trained models as online inference services or AI web applications, with heterogeneous resources, automatic scaling, one-click stress testing, canary releases, and real-time monitoring". The two things to underline:
issues.fixed: The official "EAS overview" describes it as: "deploy trained models as online inference services or AI web applications, with heterogeneous resources, automatic scaling, one-click stress testing, canary releases, and real-time monitoring". The two key points are:

issues.original: It's a **container-runtime serving layer** — your model lives in OSS, your code lives in a container image, EAS pulls the image, mounts OSS at startup, runs your start command, and listens on a port.
issues.fixed: It's a **container-runtime serving layer** — your model is in OSS, your code is in a container image, EAS pulls the image, mounts OSS at startup, runs your start command, and listens on a port.

issues.original: It's **autoscaled by replica count** — not a serverless function model (with one important exception, see below). Replicas are real GPU pods that take 30-120s to come up. Plan for that.
issues.fixed: It's **autoscaled by replica count** — not a serverless function model (with one important exception, see below). Replicas are real GPU pods that take 30-120 seconds to start. Plan accordingly.

issues.original: The four moving parts the docs call out for runtime-image deployment:
issues.fixed: The four components the docs highlight for runtime-image deployment:

issues.original: Use an official one ([CODE], [CODE]) or push your own to ACR.
issues.fixed: Use an official one ([CODE], [CODE]) or push your own to ACR.

issues.original: Decoupling them lets you update weights without rebuilding the image.
issues.fixed: Decoupling them allows you to update weights without rebuilding the image.

issues.original: at startup, EAS FUSE-mounts the OSS path you specified to a directory inside the container, e.g. [CODE].
issues.fixed: at startup, EAS FUSE-mounts the specified OSS path to a directory inside the container, e.g. [CODE].

issues.original: Do not let model paths get hardcoded to [CODE]. Switching from local-dev to EAS becomes a one-line config change instead of a code refactor.
issues.fixed: Avoid hardcoding model paths to [CODE]. Switching from local development to EAS then becomes a one-line config change rather than a code refactor.

issues.original: A practical heuristic:
issues.fixed: A practical guideline:

issues.original: You care about p99 latency.
issues.fixed: You care about p99 latency.

issues.original: The built-in queue scales replicas based on backlog, which is the right mental model for these workloads.
issues.fixed: The built-in queue scales replicas based on backlog, which is the right approach for these workloads.

issues.original: Use preemptible instances and cut the bill in half.
issues.fixed: Use preemptible instances and halve the cost.

issues.original: The console flow is:
issues.fixed: The console process is:

issues.original: If that returns a sentence, your endpoint is alive and you can go back to your colleagues looking like a wizard.
issues.fixed: If that returns a sentence, your endpoint is live, and you can return to your colleagues looking like a wizard.

issues.original: This is the part the docs do not really hammer home. Default autoscaler behaviour (scale on request rate, min replicas = 1) is a recipe for either cold-start latency tickets or surprise bills.
issues.fixed: This is the part the docs don't emphasize. Default autoscaler behavior (scaling on request rate, with a minimum of 1 replica) can lead to cold-start latency issues or unexpected bills.

issues.original: Calculate as: [CODE]. If you don't know your per-replica QPS, run the one-click stress test. The docs cover this under "Service stress testing".
issues.fixed: Calculate as: [CODE]. If you don't know your per-replica QPS, run the one-click stress test. The docs cover this under "Service stress testing".

issues.original: QPS is misleading because long generations don't show up as more requests.
issues.fixed: QPS is misleading because long generations don't register as additional requests.

issues.original: I use a 90/10 split for the first 24 hours of any model swap, then 50/50, then 0/100. If any of those steps shows degradation in the success-rate or p99 metrics, rollback is immediate — service groups change traffic weights in seconds.
issues.fixed: I use a 90/10 split for the first 24 hours of any model swap, then 50/50, and finally 0/100. If any step shows degradation in success rate or p99 metrics, rollback is immediate — service groups change traffic weights in seconds.

issues.original: Going to prod without one is the most common cause of "the model fell over at the 3pm peak" tickets.
issues.fixed: Deploying without one is the most common cause of "the model fell over at the 3pm peak" tickets.

issues.original: Buried in the docs: "If an EAS service remains in a non-Running state for 180 consecutive days, the system automatically deletes the service." Set a calendar reminder. I lost a service config once because the team that owned it dissolved and no one paid the bill. Restoring took an afternoon of re-bisecting which [CODE] version was on which weights.
issues.fixed: Buried in the docs: "If an EAS service remains in a non-Running state for 180 consecutive days, the system automatically deletes the service." Set a calendar reminder. I once lost a service config because the team that owned it dissolved and no one paid the bill. Restoring it took an afternoon of re-bisecting which [CODE] version was on which weights.

issues.original: Cold start is EAS's biggest practical problem. A vLLM Qwen3-7B container takes 60-120 s from scheduler-pick to first-token-served — model load alone is 30-60 s. If your autoscaler ever needs to add a replica under load, the first user requests in that window time out.
issues.fixed: Cold start is EAS's biggest practical issue. A vLLM Qwen3-7B container takes 60-120 seconds from scheduler pick to first token served — model load alone takes 30-60 seconds. If your autoscaler needs to add a replica under load, the first user requests in that window will time out.

issues.original: The first four articles were about the underlying primitives — DSW, DLC, EAS — that you orchestrate with Python. This one is about the two GUI products that wrap those primitives and ship a runnable thing for users who do not want to write Python: **PAI-Designer** for drag-and-drop tabular pipelines, and **Model Gallery** for zero-code open-source model deployment and fine-tuning. They are not what serious engineers reach for first, but in two specific situations they are obviously the right answer.
issues.fixed: The first four articles covered the underlying primitives — DSW, DLC, EAS — that you orchestrate with Python. This one focuses on two GUI products that wrap these primitives and provide a runnable solution for users who don't want to write Python: **PAI-Designer** for drag-and-drop tabular pipelines, and **Model Gallery** for zero-code open-source model deployment and fine-tuning. While serious engineers might not reach for them first, they are the right choice in two specific situations.

issues.original: Per the docs, Designer "implements modeling and model debugging through workflows. Users can build AI development processes by dragging and dropping different components in workflows like building blocks." The headline numbers: 140+ built-in algorithm components, exports to JSON, schedulable in DataWorks, supports custom SQL / Python / PyAlink scripts as nodes.
issues.fixed: According to the docs, Designer implements modeling and model debugging through workflows. Users can build AI development processes by dragging and dropping different components, like building blocks. Key features include 140+ built-in algorithm components, JSON export, scheduling in DataWorks, and support for custom SQL, Python, and PyAlink scripts as nodes.

issues.original: Where it shines: - **Tabular ML at MaxCompute scale.** Designer is tightly bound to MaxCompute. If your training data is a 200-million-row partitioned table on MaxCompute, Designer's built-in source / split / encode / train components run inside MaxCompute itself, not over the wire to a Python pod. You are paying for MaxCompute compute, not GPU pods sitting idle waiting on data.
issues.fixed: Where it shines: - **Tabular ML at MaxCompute scale.** Designer is tightly integrated with MaxCompute. If your training data is a 200-million-row partitioned table on MaxCompute, Designer's built-in source, split, encode, and train components run directly within MaxCompute, not over the wire to a Python pod. You pay for MaxCompute compute, not idle GPU pods waiting on data.

issues.original: Where it loses: - Anything LLM-shaped. Designer's strength is feature engineering + classical ML; it is not a place to write a custom PyTorch training loop. - Custom CUDA work, novel losses, anything where "the algorithm IS the thing".
issues.fixed: Where it loses: - Anything LLM-related. Designer excels in feature engineering and classical ML but isn't suitable for writing custom PyTorch training loops. - Custom CUDA work, novel losses, or any scenario where the algorithm is the core focus.

issues.original: Model Gallery is the tooling that wraps DLC + EAS so a non-MLOps user can fine-tune and deploy an open-source model with about six clicks. Per the docs, it "encapsulates Platform for AI (PAI)-DLC and PAI-EAS, providing a zero-code solution to efficiently deploy and train open-source large language models".
issues.fixed: Model Gallery is the tooling that wraps DLC + EAS, allowing non-MLOps users to fine-tune and deploy open-source models with just a few clicks. According to the docs, it encapsulates PAI-DLC and PAI-EAS, providing a zero-code solution for deploying and training open-source large language models.

issues.original: For fine-tuning, the docs walk through a logistics-information-extraction example: feed it a JSON dataset, pick LoRA hyperparameters from a dropdown, and it submits a DLC job for you. The Quick Start specifically calls out the **distillation pattern** — use a large teacher (Qwen3-235B) to label data and a small student (Qwen3-0.6B) to learn from it. That pattern is worth internalising; it is the single most cost-effective fine-tuning recipe I know.
issues.fixed: For fine-tuning, the docs provide a logistics-information-extraction example: feed it a JSON dataset, select LoRA hyperparameters from a dropdown, and it submits a DLC job for you. The Quick Start highlights the **distillation pattern** — using a large teacher (Qwen3-235B) to label data and a small student (Qwen3-0.6B) to learn from it. This pattern is worth internalizing; it's the most cost-effective fine-tuning method I know.

issues.original: Where Gallery shines: - **Evaluating a new model in 10 minutes.** When DeepSeek-V3 dropped, my team had it deployed and chatting in the time it took to refill a coffee. That is impossible from [CODE] if you also need to set up the OSS bucket, the security group, and the SSL cert. - **Demos for non-engineering stakeholders.** Click → endpoint → Cherry Studio chat → board meeting. - **One-click LoRA fine-tunes.** For most domain-adaptation work, the defaults the Gallery picks (LR, epochs, LoRA rank) are within 5% of optimal.
issues.fixed: Where Gallery shines: - **Evaluating a new model in 10 minutes.** When DeepSeek-V3 was released, my team deployed and tested it in the time it took to refill a coffee. This is impossible with [CODE] if you also need to set up the OSS bucket, security group, and SSL cert. - **Demos for non-engineering stakeholders.** Click → endpoint → Cherry Studio chat → board meeting. - **One-click LoRA fine-tunes.** For most domain-adaptation work, the defaults Gallery picks (LR, epochs, LoRA rank) are within 5% of optimal.

issues.original: Where Gallery loses: - Custom architectures. If you've modified the model code, you need DSW + DLC. - Tight latency targets. The defaults Gallery picks for serving are sensible, not optimised. If you need <100ms p99, you're going to want to write the EAS deployment yourself with the right batching config. - Air-gapped or cross-region deploys. Gallery assumes "deploy in the region you're in".
issues.fixed: Where Gallery loses: - Custom architectures. If you've modified the model code, you need DSW + DLC. - Tight latency targets. The defaults Gallery picks for serving are sensible but not optimized. If you need <100ms p99, you'll need to write the EAS deployment yourself with the right batching config. - Air-gapped or cross-region deploys. Gallery assumes deployment in the current region.

issues.original: Summary heuristic: **start as high up the stack as the requirements allow**. Most teams over-engineer day one — they build a custom DLC + EAS pipeline for what is really a Model Gallery deploy. Optimise for time-to-first-token, then refactor down once you have real traffic and real metrics to design against.
issues.fixed: Summary heuristic: **start as high up the stack as the requirements allow**. Many teams over-engineer on day one, building custom DLC + EAS pipelines when a Model Gallery deploy would suffice. Optimize for time-to-first-token, then refactor once you have real traffic and metrics to design against.
